source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
title: Ensure Compute Instances Are Launched With Shielded VM Enabled

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure Compute Instances Are Launched With Shielded VM Enabled
input_row.description: To defend against advanced threats and ensure that the boot loader and firmware on your VMs are signed and untampered, it is recommended that Compute instances are launched with Shielded VM enabled.
input_row.rationale: Shielded VMs are virtual machines (VMs) on Google Cloud Platform hardened by a set of security controls that help defend against rootkits and bootkits. Shielded VM offers verifiable integrity of your Compute Engine VM instances, so you can be confident your instances haven't been compromised by boot- or kernel-level malware or rootkits. Shielded VM's verifiable integrity is achieved through the use of Secure Boot, virtual trusted platform module (vTPM)-enabled Measured Boot, and integrity monitoring. Shielded VM instances run firmware which is signed and verified using Google's Certificate Authority, ensuring that the instance's firmware is unmodified and establishing the root of trust for Secure Boot. Integrity monitoring helps you understand and make decisions about the state of your VM instances and the Shielded VM vTPM enables Measured Boot by performing the measurements needed to create a known good boot baseline, called the integrity policy baseline. The integrity policy baseline is used for comparison with measurements from subsequent VM boots to determine if anything has changed. Secure Boot helps ensure that the system only runs authentic software by verifying the digital signature of all boot components, and halting the boot process if signature verification fails.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the VM instances page by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/compute/instances. 2. Click on the instance name to see its VM instance details page. 3. Under the section Shielded VM, ensure that vTPM and Integrity Monitoring are on.  From Google Cloud CLI 1. For each instance in your project, get its metadata: gcloud compute instances list --format=json | jq -r '. | "vTPM: \(.[].shieldedInstanceConfig.enableVtpm) IntegrityMonitoring: \(.[].shieldedInstanceConfig.enableIntegrityMonitoring) Name: \(.[].name)"' 2. Ensure that there is a shieldedInstanceConfig configuration and that configuration has the enableIntegrityMonitoring and enableVtpm set to true. If the VM is not a Shield VM image, you will not see a shieldedInstanceConfig` in the output.
input_row.remediation: To be able turn on Shielded VM on an instance, your instance must use an image with Shielded VM support. From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the VM instances page by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/compute/instances. 2. Click on the instance name to see its VM instance details page. 3. Click STOP to stop the instance. 4. When the instance has stopped, click EDIT. 5. In the Shielded VM section, select Turn on vTPM and Turn on Integrity Monitoring. 6. Optionally, if you do not use any custom or unsigned drivers on the instance, also select Turn on Secure Boot. 7. Click the Save button to modify the instance and then click START to restart it. From Google Cloud CLI You can only enable Shielded VM options on instances that have Shielded VM support. For a list of Shielded VM public images, run the gcloud compute images list command with the following flags: gcloud compute images list --project gce-uefi-images --no-standard-images 1. Stop the instance: gcloud compute instances stop <INSTANCE_NAME> 2. Update the instance: gcloud compute instances update <INSTANCE_NAME> --shielded-vtpm --shielded- vm-integrity-monitoring  3. Optionally, if you do not use any custom or unsigned drivers on the instance, also turn on secure boot. gcloud compute instances update <INSTANCE_NAME> --shielded-vm-secure-boot 4. Restart the instance: gcloud compute instances start <INSTANCE_NAME> Prevention: You can ensure that all new VMs will be created with Shielded VM enabled by setting up an Organization Policy to for Shielded VM at https://console.cloud.google.com/iam- admin/orgpolicies/compute-requireShieldedVm. Learn more at: https://cloud.google.com/security/shielded-cloud/shielded-vm#organization-policy- constraint. Default Value: By default, Compute Instances do not have Shielded VM enabled. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/modifying-shielded-vm 2. https://cloud.google.com/shielded-vm 3. https://cloud.google.com/security/shielded-cloud/shielded-vm#organization- policy-constraint Additional Information: If you do use custom or unsigned drivers on the instance, enabling Secure Boot will cause the machine to no longer boot. Turn on Secure Boot only on instances that have been verified to not have any custom drivers installed.
input_row.references: 1. https://cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/modifying-shielded-vm 2. https://cloud.google.com/shielded-vm 3. https://cloud.google.com/security/shielded-cloud/shielded-vm#organization- policy-constraint Additional Information: If you do use custom or unsigned drivers on the instance, enabling Secure Boot will cause the machine to no longer boot. Turn on Secure Boot only on instances that have been verified to not have any custom drivers installed.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control can be fully verified via GCP APIs or the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud). The audit details provided explicitly mention using the `gcloud` command to list and verify the Shielded VM settings of compute instances, such as `enableVtpm` and `enableIntegrityMonitoring`. These are standard GCP service properties, and the `gcloud compute instances list` command directly retrieves the relevant configuration settings for Shielded VMs. Hence, this control is suitable for automated checking.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_compute_instance_shielded_vm_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **GCP API/SDK Methods**: Use `gcloud compute instances list` to fetch compute instances' data.
- **CLI Command**: Execute `gcloud compute instances list --format=json | jq -r '. | \"vTPM: \\(.[].shieldedInstanceConfig.enableVtpm) IntegrityMonitoring: \\(.[].shieldedInstanceConfig.enableIntegrityMonitoring) Name: \\(.[].name)\"'` to retrieve and confirm Shielded VM settings.
- **Resources to Enumerate**: All compute instances within the GCP project.
- **Configuration Properties to Validate**: The presence of `shieldedInstanceConfig` block and its properties `enableVtpm` and `enableIntegrityMonitoring` set to true.
- **Pass/Fail Criteria**: 
  - **Pass**: The `shieldedInstanceConfig` exists, and both `enableVtpm` and `enableIntegrityMonitoring` are set to **true** for the instance.
  - **Fail**: The `shieldedInstanceConfig` does not exist, or either `enableVtpm` or `enableIntegrityMonitoring` is not set to **true**.

By executing this automated check, organizations can efficiently ensure compliance with the Shielded VM requirement for their compute instances across their GCP environment.
step2_review:
---
## 1. APPROACH VALIDATION: **AGREE**

- **Manual vs Automated Classification:**  
  The classification as "Automated" is correct. Shielded VM settings are exposed via the Compute Engine API and are retrievable using the `gcloud` CLI and REST API. No manual inspection is required to determine compliance.

- **GCP API/SDK/CLI Coverage:**  
  The `gcloud compute instances list` command (with appropriate formatting) and the Compute Engine API's `instances.list` method both return the `shieldedInstanceConfig` object for each VM, including `enableVtpm` and `enableIntegrityMonitoring`.  
  GCP Config (now called Config Validator) can also be used for policy-as-code, but is not required for this check.

- **Other Considerations:**  
  CloudFormation is AWS-specific and not relevant here.

---

## 2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY: **Score: 8/10**

- **Automation Details:**  
  - The use of `gcloud compute instances list --format=json` is valid and returns the required fields.
  - The provided `jq` command attempts to parse the output for the two Shielded VM settings and the instance name.

- **Areas for Improvement:**  
  - The `jq` command assumes all instances have the `shieldedInstanceConfig` field, but pre-existing VMs or non-Shielded VMs may not have this field, resulting in errors or incomplete output.  
  - The command does not enumerate across all zones by default. `gcloud compute instances list` only lists instances in the default zone unless `--all-zones` is specified.
  - The program name (`gcp_compute_instance_shielded_vm_enabled`) is clear, but could be more descriptive by specifying both required properties (e.g., `vtpm_integrity_monitoring`).

- **Program Name Convention:**  
  - Follows the convention, but could be improved for clarity and specificity.

---

## 3. IMPROVEMENTS

- **Command Robustness:**  
  - Use `gcloud compute instances list --format=json --all-zones` to ensure all instances are checked, not just those in the default zone.
  - Update the `jq` parsing to handle instances without the `shieldedInstanceConfig` field gracefully, e.g.:
    ```bash
    gcloud compute instances list --format=json --all-zones | \
      jq -r '.[] | "Name: \(.name) vTPM: \(.shieldedInstanceConfig.enableVtpm // "N/A") IntegrityMonitoring: \(.shieldedInstanceConfig.enableIntegrityMonitoring // "N/A")"'
    ```
  - Alternatively, use the REST API directly for large-scale environments or integrate with Security Command Center findings.

- **Missing Considerations:**  
  - The audit should clarify that only VMs created after Shielded VM became default (mid-2020) will have these fields set by default; older VMs may lack them.
  - Consider checking for all three Shielded VM features (`enableSecureBoot`, `enableVtpm`, `enableIntegrityMonitoring`), as Secure Boot is also a Shielded VM feature (though not always required by CIS).
  - If using automation, recommend periodic scheduled checks and integration with alerting.

- **Better Program Name Suggestion:**  
  - `gcp_compute_instance_shielded_vm_vtpm_integrity_enabled`
  - Or, if Secure Boot is also checked: `gcp_compute_instance_shielded_vm_all_features_enabled`

---

## 4. CONFIDENCE: **HIGH**

- The assessment is technically sound, leverages GCP APIs/SDK correctly, and is automatable. Minor improvements are needed for completeness and robustness.

---

## 5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION: **Modify**

- **Keep the approach as Automated.**
- **Modify** the automation details to:
  - Use `--all-zones` to ensure full coverage.
  - Update the parsing logic to handle missing fields gracefully.
  - Optionally, clarify in documentation that older VMs may not have Shielded VM features enabled by default.
  - Consider a more descriptive program name as suggested above.

---

### **Summary Table**

| Section             | Verdict / Score | Notes                                                                                 |
|---------------------|-----------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Approach Validation | AGREE           | Correctly classified as Automated; GCP APIs/SDK fully support this check.             |
| Technical Accuracy  | 8/10            | Command works, but needs zone coverage and error handling for missing fields.          |
| Improvements        |                 | Use `--all-zones`, handle missing fields, clarify for older VMs, improve naming.       |
| Confidence          | HIGH            | GCP supports this check robustly; minor technical improvements needed.                 |
| Final Recommendation| Modify          | Update automation details and program name for completeness and clarity.               |

---
